result:
The window is cold against my forehead. Outside, the world is not doing anything remarkable. A grey squirrel flicks its tail on a power line. A car passes, the sound of its engine rising and falling like a slow breath. I am counting the words in my head, and I have 1000 of them. It feels like a fortune, a heavy sack of coins. What do I buy with them? Do I build a small, perfect house? Do I try to tell you everything? Or do I simply spend them, one by one, watching them drop into the well of this page, listening for an echo that may never come?

It starts, always, with the mundane. The click of the keyboard, the taste of tea gone lukewarm. The faint ache in my right shoulder from a sleep spent chasing something in a dream I can no longer recall. This is the clay. From this, we are supposed to sculpt gods and monsters, philosophies and love stories. But maybe the truth is in the clay itself. Maybe the miracle isn’t the statue, but the fact that there was earth to dig from at all.

I think about the 1000 words as 1000 steps. A walk. If I walk 1000 steps from my front door, where do I end up? At the edge of the park, by the old oak with the split trunk. I know this. I’ve measured my life in such walks. A thousand steps to the bakery, two thousand to the river, five thousand to the place where my old school used to be. Words are steps through a landscape of thought. You can wander aimlessly, or you can march with purpose. Today, I think I’ll wander.

Memory is a trickster. It hands you a perfect, sun-drenched afternoon—the smell of cut grass, the drone of a lawnmower, the cool linoleum of a childhood kitchen. But it withholds the reason you were sad that day. It shows you the face of someone you once loved, but it blurs the exact shade of their eyes. It offers the soundtrack but loses the script. So we spend our words trying to reconstruct the lost sets, to paint the missing backdrops. We write to remember, but even more, we write to complete.

There is a pressure in having a finite amount. 1000 words. Not 999, not 1001. A boundary. I find I crave boundaries. A blank page is a terror; a page with a limit is a challenge. It’s the difference between staring at the ocean and being handed a bucket. “Bring me back something interesting,” the page says. I dip my bucket into the swirling mess of consciousness and hope I catch more than just salty water.

I think of all the things that are happening right now, as my fingers find these letters. A bee is drowning in someone’s soda can. A surgeon is sighing with relief. A glacier is calving, with a sound too deep for human ears. Someone is reading a sentence that changes their mind. Someone else is forgetting their mother’s voice. The planet is spinning, the universe expanding, and here I am, arranging these 26 symbols into patterns, hoping they mean something. It is an act of profound faith, or profound foolishness. Perhaps they are the same.

What do I owe you, who might read this? Do I owe you a point? A moral? A neatly tied bow? Or is the gift simply the sharing of a consciousness, for a moment? A voice in your head that is not your own, saying, “Look, I noticed the squirrel too. I also feel the ache. You are not alone in your noticing.” Maybe that’s all any of us want—to have our private catalog of observations validated by another soul. *Yes*, we say with our words, *I saw that thing too. It was real.*

The tyranny of the profound. The feeling that with 1000 words, one must aim for the stars. But what if the most profound thing is the refusal to be profound? What if it’s all in the grain of the wood, the crack in the pavement, the way the light slants at 4 PM in October? The ordinary, attended to with fierce, quiet focus, becomes extraordinary. The snail’s trail on the porch. The specific weight of a well-made book. The silence between two people when no more words are needed.

I am running out of words now. The sack is lighter. I’ve spent them on squirrels and tea, on memories and universes. I haven’t built the perfect house, but I’ve perhaps laid out some stones, traced a possible path. That’s all writing is, really: path-making. You leave marks so that others, or even your future self, can follow. Or so that you can find your way back.

And what of the last words? The final dozen or so? They feel precious, weighted. Do I tell you I love you? Do I impart some hard-won wisdom? The wisdom is: there is no wisdom. Only the looking, the noting, the trying to connect.

The sky outside the window has changed. The grey has lifted to a soft, pearly blue. The squirrel is gone. The world has continued, indifferent to my 1000 words. And that’s as it should be. The power doesn’t lie in changing the world with them. The power is in the act itself. In the choosing, the ordering, the offering.

Here is the last one.
Here.
Is.
The.
Last…
One.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
